Excuse me, I need to get to the Museum of Modern Art. What is the fastest way from here?
At this hour? The subway is faster than a taxi, for sure. The roads are more crowded right now -- there is real gridlock on 5th.
The subway it is. Is the express quicker than the local?
Much quicker. Take the express and it is the most reliable ride you will get. Just avoid the platform at the far end -- it is the most crowded one.
Good to know. And on the way back, I would love a nicer view. Any ideas?
Then take the ferry. It is slower, but it is by far the most scenic route, and it is less crowded than the trains.
Perfect. So the fastest way there, the prettiest way back. Thank you so much!
You got it. One last tip: if you drive later, the bridge is cheaper than the tunnel -- the toll is lower this week. Enjoy the city!
The ferry is the most scenic route.
Walking is the cheapest option of all.
What is the fastest way to get to Central Park?
The subway is faster than a taxi at rush hour.
Could you drop me off at the corner, please?
The ferry is the most scenic route back.
Which do you think is the best way across town?

Good morning, and here is your nine o'clock traffic and transit update for New York City. It is rush hour, and things are busier than usual. On the roads, downtown is more crowded than uptown right now, and there is heavy gridlock near the bridges, so a taxi is the slowest option this morning. If you are heading south, the subway is by far the fastest and most reliable way to travel. Trains are running on time, and the express is quicker than the local, as always. For anyone near the water, the ferry is the most scenic route, and it is far less crowded than the trains today. A quick tip: the toll on the tunnel is higher than the bridge this week, so if you are driving, the bridge is the cheaper choice. Whatever you choose, leave a little earlier than usual. Have a smooth trip.

Here is a short tip for comparing your options when you travel. When you compare two things, use a comparative. For short words, add e-r and than, like the subway is faster than the bus, or the walk is longer than the ride. For longer words, use more or less before the word, like a taxi is more expensive than the train, and the ferry is less crowded than the subway. When you compare three or more things and pick the top one, use a superlative with the. For short words, add e-s-t, like the subway is the fastest, and for longer words, use the most or the least, like the ferry is the most scenic. And two quick ones you just have to remember: good becomes better and the best, and bad becomes worse and the worst. Get these right, and you will always choose the smartest way to get around.
